title: Enhancing Blood Cells Classification using Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks
authors: Guilherme Cruz, Nouhaila Innan, Alberto Marchisio, Gabriel Falcao, Muhammad Shafique
abstract: Accurate classification of microscopic blood cells is still a critical task in medical image analysis, where subtle variations and limited data can challenge conventional deep learning models. As such, we investigate in this work the potential of Hybrid Quantum-Classical Neural Networks (HQNNs) to enhance feature representation and improve classification performance in this domain. We propose a modular architecture combining a pre-trained ResNet-50 backbone with a low-dimensional latent bottleneck and a variational quantum circuit, enabling a direct comparison between quantum-enhanced and purely classical transformation mechanisms. To isolate the contribution of the quantum component, we evaluate three architectures: a HQNN model, a Classical Matched Model with an additional nonlinear transformation layer of comparable capacity, and a baseline model without an intermediate transformation stage. Experiments conducted on two publicly available blood cell datasets, namely the Blood Cell Images dataset and the PBC dataset, demonstrate that HQNNs consistently achieve superior or more balanced performance across evaluation metrics. In the Blood Cell Images Dataset, the proposed approach improves macro F1-score by up to 3.7% compared to classical baselines, while improving the F1-score from 98.54% to 98.69% in the more challenging 8-class scenario with near-saturated performance. Additional evaluation on IBM quantum hardware shows that the model remains robust under noise, with only a modest performance degradation relative to simulated results. These results indicate that quantum feature transformations can enhance discriminative representations, particularly in challenging classification scenarios, and highlight the practical potential of HQNN models for medical imaging tasks.
summary: This paper demonstrates that Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks can enhance the classification of complex medical images, such as blood cells, by providing superior feature representation. The researchers show that the quantum component improves the separability of visually similar cell types and remains robust even when executed on noisy IBM quantum hardware.
main_problem: The difficulty of accurately classifying microscopic blood cells due to subtle morphological variations and high intra-class similarity in medical imaging.
main_result: Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks (HQNNs) outperform both classical baseline and parameter-matched classical models, specifically improving macro F1-scores by up to 3.7% in challenging classes.
method: A hybrid pipeline using a pre-trained ResNet-50 backbone for feature extraction, followed by a dimensionality reduction layer and a variational quantum circuit (VQC) for quantum-enhanced feature transformation.
model_or_system: A 10-qubit variational quantum circuit using angle encoding (RY rotations) and a ring-structured CNOT entangling pattern, implemented via PennyLane and tested on both simulators and IBM quantum hardware.
key_observables: Accuracy, macro F1-score, precision, recall, and multiclass ROC-AUC.
important_parameters: 10 qubits, 4 variational layers, 2048-dimensional feature vector reduced to a 10-dimensional latent bottleneck.
main_assumptions: The performance gains are attributed to quantum feature transformations rather than simple increases in parameter count; the study assumes the utility of the NISQ-era hardware for such tasks despite noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows blood cell classes; Figure 2 is a pipeline flowchart; Figure 3 is a VQC schematic; Figures 4-5 show training convergence; Figures 6-12 present performance comparisons, confusion matrices, and per-class F1-scores.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the medical problem, describes the HQNN architecture and comparison framework, details the experimental setup on datasets and hardware, presents performance results across different models and data scales, and concludes with limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Representative examples of the eight classes in the PBC dataset.

Fig. 2. Overview of the proposed blood cell classification pipeline. The framework processes both the Blood Cell Images and PBC datasets through a common preprocessing and augmentation stage, followed by feature extraction using a pre-trained ResNet-50 backbone. The resulting 2048-dimensional feature vector is reduced to a 10-dimensional latent representation and passed to an HQNN composed of angle encoding, a variational quantum circuit with 10 qubits and 4 layers, and Pauli-Z measurement. The resulting quantum feature vector is then fed to a lightweight classification head for final blood cell type prediction.
Fig. 4. Training and validation loss curves of the HQNN model on the Blood Cell Images Dataset (4 Classes).
Fig. 6. Comparison of overall accuracy and macro F1-score for the evaluated models on the Blood Cell Images Dataset.
Fig. 5. Training and validation accuracy curves of the HQNN model on the Blood Cell Images Dataset.
